coredata.dc:title: Bi-color FRET from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor: A universal aptasensing platform for simultaneous determination of dual targets
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Jing
Cui, Haining
Lu, Xiaoting
Wang, Chengquan
An, Keqi
Hao, Nan
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Emerging nano-donor and nano-receptor FRET pairs are more advantageous to broad biosensing applications. However, those reported FRET system generally containing a single pair cannot afford multiplexed analysis. Herein, bi-color FRET from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor has been adopted to fabricate a simple-to-use aptasensor for simultaneous determination of dual targets in a single run. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and ochratoxin A (OTA) were selected as the analytes because they have synergistic effect to induce enhanced toxicity. Specially, carbon dots (CDs) and CdZnTe quantum dots (QDs), with unambiguous separation of emission peaks, were used as nano-donors to label the aptamers specific for AFB1 and OTA, respectively. These single-stranded aptamers with labels could be stably adsorbed on a single nano-acceptor MoS2 nanosheets surface due to van der Waals force, leading to the occurrence of FRET. When the aptasensor incubated with AFB1 and OTA, the specific binding between aptamers and targets resulted in partial release of the nano-donor labeled aptamers which disturb the FRET process and ultimately led to the fluorescence recovery to some extent. This aptasensor exhibited a wide detection range of 0.01–10 ng mL−1 for AFB1 and 0.02–5 ng mL−1 for OTA with low detection limits (3.3 pg mL−1 for AFB1 and 7.1 pg mL−1 for OTA). This aptasensor provides a new avenue for simultaneous determination of dual mycotoxins due to its advantages of simple operation, good selectivity and high sensitivity. By simply changing the specific aptamer, our strategy will certainly provide a universalaptasensing platform in advancing multiplexed analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
MoS2 nanosheets
Aptasensing platform
Simultaneous determination
Aflatoxin B1
Ochratoxin A

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and CdZnTe QDs
Preparation of CDs/apt1 and QDs/apt2 signal tags
Fabrication of the aptasensing platform and the procedure for detection
Characterization of CDs and CdZnTe QDs
Characterization of CDs/apt1 and QDs/apt2 signal tags
Fabrication of the aptasensing platform and its working mechanism
Simultaneously detecting AFB1 and OTA in a single run

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank for the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21976071 and 21675066 ), Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. SATM201807 ), Jiangsu University Fund (No. 19JDG025 ), and Cultivation Fund of Young Key Teacher at Jiangsu University (2015).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of MoS2-nanosheet suspension. Inset: Photograph of MoS2-nanosheet suspension (a) and TEM image of MoS2 nanosheets (b). (B) FTIR spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum (a) and fluorescence spectrum (b) of CDs. Inset: Photographs of CDs solution taken under visible-light (a) and UV illumination at 365 nm (b). (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum (a) and fluorescence spectrum (b) of CdZnTe QDs. Inset: Photographs of CdZnTe QDs solution taken under visible-light (a) and UV illumination at 365 nm (b). TEM images of (E) CDs and (F) CdZnTe QDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of free apt1 (a) and CDs/apt1 (b) along with fluorescence spectrum of CDs/apt1 (c). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of free apt2 (a) and QDs/apt2 (b) along with fluorescence spectrum of QDs/apt2 (c). (C) The spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets (a) and fluorescence spectrum of CDs/CdZnTe QDs mixture (b).
(A) FTIR spectra for CDs (a) and CDs/apt1 signal tags (b). (B) FTIR spectra for free MPA (a), MPA-CdZnTe QDs (b), and QDs/apt2 signal tags (c).

(A) Schematic illustration of the aptasensor fabrication and sensing mechanism. Fluorescence spectra of the mixture of CDs/apt1 and QDs/apt2 (B), CDs/apt1-MoS2-QDs/apt2 bioconjugations before (C) and after (D) incubation with 20 ng mL−1 of AFB1 and 20 ng mL−1 of OTA.

(A) The fluorescence intensity of different volume ratio of (a) MoS2 and CDs/apt1 and (b) MoS2 and QDs/apt2. (B) Effect of the adsorption time between MoS2 and CDs/apt1 (a) and between MoS2 and QDs/apt2 (b). (C) Effect of the binding time for determination of AFB1 (a) and OTA (b). (D) Selectivity study of aptasensor, G1 to G4 representing the fluorescence response when the aptasensor incubated with AFB1/OTA mixture, AFB1/FB1 mixture, AFB2/OTA mixture, and AFB2/FB1 mixture. (E) Normalized FL intensity of aptasensor at different AFB1 concentrations (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10 and 20 ng mL−1) and different OTA concentrations (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10 and 20 ng mL−1). (F) The relationships between the plot of normalized FL intensity and the concentrations of AFB1 (a) and OTA (b).
Comparison of the proposed aptasensor with those reported fluorescence immuosensors and aptasensors for AFB1 and/or OTA determination.
Results of simultaneous determination of AFB1 and OTA in spiked peanut samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Bi-color FRET from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor: A universal aptasensing platform for simultaneous determination of dual targets

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China (K. Wang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Emerging nano-donor and nano-receptor FRET pairs are more advantageous to broad biosensing applications. However, those reported FRET system generally containing a single pair cannot afford multiplexed analysis. Herein, bi-color FRET from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor has been adopted to fabricate a simple-to-use aptasensor for simultaneous determination of dual targets in a single run. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and ochratoxin A (OTA) were selected as the analytes because they have synergistic effect to induce enhanced toxicity. Specially, carbon dots (CDs) and CdZnTe quantum dots (QDs), with unambiguous separation of emission peaks, were used as nano-donors to label the aptamers specific for AFB1 and OTA, respectively. These single-stranded aptamers with labels could be stably adsorbed on a single nano-acceptor MoS2 nanosheets surface due to van der Waals force, leading to the occurrence of FRET. When the aptasensor incubated with AFB1 and OTA, the specific binding between aptamers and targets resulted in partial release of the nano-donor labeled aptamers which disturb the FRET process and ultimately led to the fluorescence recovery to some extent. This aptasensor exhibited a wide detection range of 0.01–10 ng mL−1 for AFB1 and 0.02–5 ng mL−1 for OTA with low detection limits (3.3 pg mL−1 for AFB1 and 7.1 pg mL−1 for OTA). This aptasensor provides a new avenue for simultaneous determination of dual mycotoxins due to its advantages of simple operation, good selectivity and high sensitivity. By simply changing the specific aptamer, our strategy will certainly provide a universalaptasensing platform in advancing multiplexed analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) presents as a very promising tool for investigation of molecular interactions and molecular structure [1–3]. Two essential requirements for FRET occurrence are 1) a good spectral overlap for acceptor absorption and donor emission and 2) a close distance (<10 nm) between acceptor and donor [1]. Fortunately, separate donors and acceptors can be brought in close proximity through binding events such as protein interaction, antibody-based immunoreaction, and DNA hybridization [2,4]. Disturbing the molecular interactions by the specific targets will lead to spatial separation of donor and acceptor probes which make donor–acceptor pair cannot engage in FRET process. As a result, FRET strategy becomes particular prevailing in biosensing owing to its target-specific signal and homogeneous assays without labor-intensive washing and separation steps [4]. In these designs, FRET assays can significantly improve the duration and simplicity of the experiment, in particular, can effectively avoid “false positive” results. Organic fluorophores are commonly served as either energy donor or energy acceptor in traditional FRET systems, for instance, Rhodamine B and methyl-red [5], Pyrene excimer-SYBR Green I [6], and Cy3-Cy5 [7] pairs. However, the optical limitations of organic fluorophores such as photobleaching, low resistance to chemicals, as well as short fluorescent lifetime, have seriously limited their broad biosensing applications [4].

Employing nanomaterials with appropriate optical features, to replace the traditional donor–acceptor pair, is certainly a central strategy to address the limitations of organic fluorophores. As a typical example, CdTe quantum dots (QDs)-Au nanoparticles (NPs), adopted as nano-donor and nano-acceptor pair, has aided advances in assembling biosensors for various targets ranged from small molecules [8,9] to proteins [10,11]. In most recent years, a cascade of FRET assays has been designed based on different nano-donor and nano-acceptor pairs like graphene QDs (GQDs)-AuNPs [12], GQDs-carbon nanotubes [13], carbon dots (CDs)-Au nanoclusters [14,15], CDs-MnO2 nanostructures [16], GQDs-porphyrinic metal-organic frameworks [17], Au nanoclusters-MnO2 nanosheets [18], upconversion nanoparticles respectively paired to Ti3C2 nanosheets [19], Au nanorods [20], AuNPs [21] or ZnS NPs [22]. However, all these systems based on a single FRET pair are generally suitable for a single target detection in one measurement. Apparently, bi-color or multi-color FRET biosensors with an unambiguous separation of different nano-donor’s emission, if available, might provide a forceful technical support in advancing multiplexed analysis in a single measurement.

Mycotoxins, a group of toxic secondary metabolites generated by filamentous fungi, possess lethal threats to human or animals when they are appeared in food/feed [23,24]. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), possessing the highest toxicity among various mycotoxins, has been defined as a group I carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) in 1993 [25]. Ochratoxin A (OTA), listed as group IIB carcinogen by IARC, is another toxic mycotoxin which can be found in various foodstuffs [25]. What makes it worse is that the co-existence of AFB1 and OTA in a single sample leading to an enhanced toxicity owing to synergistic effect [26,27]. Chromatographic methodologies including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [28] and HPLC or liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry [29,30] are primarily used for multi-mycotoxin detection. However, these chromatographic methods suffer from expensive instruments, well-trained professionals and tedious procedures, which limit their applications in practice [31]. Thus, developing a sensitive, accurate, and rapid method for multi-mycotoxin detection is significant for food safety monitoring.

In this work, a simple-to-use aptasensor based on bi-color FRET from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor has been developed for simultaneous detection of AFB1 and OTA. Carbon dots (CDs) and CdZnTe QDs were regarded as two different nano-donors of FRET process, to label the respective aptamer against AFB1 and OTA. In another aspect, MoS2 nanosheets were selected as a single nano-acceptor paired to CDs and CdZnTe QDs. The van der Waals force between single-stranded aptamers and MoS2 nanosheets could bring the donor-acceptor pairs in near field and caused the energy transfer from donors to acceptor occur. By taking advantages of bi-color FRET from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor and target-induced fluorescence recovery, AFB1 and OTA can be simultaneously quantified in a single run.

We obtained AFB1, aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), fumonisin B1 (FB1), and ochratoxin A (OTA) from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), and other chemicals were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). The aptamers possessing sequences of 5′-NH2-TGG GGT TTT GGT GGC GGG TGG TGT ACG GGC GAG GG-3′ (apt1, against AFB1) and 5′-NH2-GAT CGG GTG TGG GTG GCG TAA AGG GAG CAT CGG ACA-3′ (apt2, against OTA) were synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) was used for aptasensor fabrication and detection procedure. We used deionized water to conduct all experiments.

A modified liquid exfoliation method with a little modification was used to prepare MoS2 nanosheets [32,33]. Firstly, MoS2 powder (300 mg) was added to a mixture of ethanol and water (45% v/v) in a flask (250 mL), which was sonicated for 24 h to produce a dark green suspension. We collected the supernatant after centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The collected supernatant was further centrifugated at 12000 rpm for 30 min and the precipitation was baked at 70 °C in oven. Finally, the as-prepared MoS2 nanosheets were dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and CdZnTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: A reported hydrothermal method was adopted to synthesize CDs [34] while CdZnTe QDs was prepared according to our reported procedure [35]. The details for both syntheses were described in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs/apt1 and QDs/apt2 signal tags
ce:section.ce:para: To produce apt1-coupled CDs (CDs/apt1) and apt2-coupled CdZnTe QDs (QDs/apt2) signal tags, 100 μL of EDC and 100 μL of NHS (both had a concentration of 200 μM, dissolved in 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4)) was mixed with 5 mL of CDs (1.5 mg mL−1), followed by 2-h stirring to activate carboxyl groups. After that, 20 μL of 100 μM of apt1 was added to the mixture which was stirred for another 24 h at room temperature. Finally, the resultant CDs/apt1 signal tags were dispersed in 5 mL Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C refrigerator for later use. The QDs/apt2 signal tags were acquired through a similar operation except for the difference of fluorescence indicator (CdZnTe QDs (5 mL, 3.5 mg mL−1)) and apt2 (20 μL, 100 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the aptasensing platform and the procedure for detection
ce:section.ce:para: CDs/apt1 (100 μL) and QDs/apt2 (100 μL) were added to 350 μL of MoS2 nanosheets (2.5 mg mL−1), followed by 6-min incubation under the room temperature. The mixture was diluted to 600 μL by 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). Then the as-obtained bioconjugations (CDs/apt1-MoS2-QDs/apt2) were employed as the aptasensor which were then incubated with AFB1 (20 μL) and OTA (20 μL) with different concentrations under room temperature for 30 min. A single excitation wavelength of 380 nm was used to record the fluorescence intensity of mixed suspension for analysis.

The apparatus information for characterizations such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum were displayed in Supporting Information.

The as-prepared MoS2 nanosheets appeared to be black green in color (Inset a, Fig. 1 A). The UV–vis absorption spectrum for MoS2 nanosheets suspension showed four characteristic absorption peaks, which located at 403, 450, 610, and 670 nm, respectively (Fig. 1A) [32,36]. Two peaks at 610 and 670 nm can be respectively attributed to B and A excitonic peaks, resulting from the K point of the Brillouin zone in two-dimensional MoS2 nanosheet having large lateral dimensions [37]. The other two absorption bands at 403 and 450 nm are ascribed to the direct excitonic transition, from the deep valence to the conduction band [36]. The morphology of MoS2 was checked by TEM, from which we can notice the plane size for MoS2 nanosheets ranged between 100 nm and 200 nm (Inset b, Fig. 1A). This TEM image provided a direct evidence for a sheet-structured MoS2. The bulk MoS2 was successfully stripped into sheets, which could provide a large surface area for biosorption and other reactions [38]. Corresponding FTIR spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets has been displayed as Fig. 1B, a characteristic peak around 480 cm−1 was ascribed to Mo-S vibration. The peak observed at 3423 cm−1 was resulted from O-H stretching vibrations. Two absorption peaks appeared at 1100 and 1398 cm−1 were attributed to the sulfate vibrations [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and CdZnTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the optical properties, both UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded for CDs and CdZnTe QDs. UV–vis spectrum for CDs displayed a characteristic absorption band at 340 nm (curve a, Fig. 1C), demonstrating they had relatively uniform particle size [34]. The fluorescence spectrum for CDs displayed an emission peak located at 441 nm under the excitation of 380 nm (curve b, Fig. 1C). The aqueously dispersed CDs appeared as a clear solution under visible light (photograph a, inset of Fig. 1C) and showed strong blue fluorescence under UV illumination at 365 nm (photograph b, inset of Fig. 1C). For CdZnTe QDs, their UV–vis absorption peak was observed at 601 nm (curve a, Fig. 1D) while the emission peak appeared at 645 nm (curve b, Fig. 1D). Moreover, CdZnTe QDs exhibited jacinth and red under visible light (photograph a, inset of Fig. 1D) and UV illumination at 365 nm (photograph b, inset of Fig. 1D), respectively. Furthermore, the morphologies of the as-obtained CDs (Fig. 1E) and QDs (Fig. 1F) were investigated by TEM images. As noted, both CDs and CdZnTe QDs were closed to sphere and had an average diameter of 4.1 nm and 2.3 nm, respectively. Both of them had good dispersity and narrow size distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs/apt1 and QDs/apt2 signal tags
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, aptamers were linked to the surface of CDs and CdZnTe QDs using the carbodiimide chemistry between amino groups in aptamers and carboxylic groups on CDs and QDs surface, with the help of EDC/NHS [41]. As we can see, the free apt1 showed a characteristic peak at 260 nm (curve a, Fig. 2 A) while the resultant CDs/apt1 exhibited two UV–vis peaks at 260 nm and 345 nm (curve b, Fig. 2A). When benchmarked against the UV–vis spectrum of the CDs (curve a, Fig. 1C), the peak of resultant CDs/apt1 has a slight red-shift of 5 nm. The coupling of apt1 on CDs did not influence the fluorescence property of CDs. As indicated, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs/apt1 (curve c, Fig. 2A) was recorded which displayed a strong fluorescence emission at 447 nm, having 6-nm red shift when benchmarked against the emission peak of free CDs (curve b, Fig. 1C). This 6-nm red shift might originate from the biomolecular conjugation on the surface of CDs [42], providing another evidence for the successful preparation of CDs/apt1.

To verify the successful assembly between apt2 and CdZnTe QDs, UV–vis absorption spectra for both free apt2 and QDs/apt2 were also recorded. It can be seen that the free apt2 and CdZnTe QDs exhibited their respective absorption peak at 260 nm (curve a, Fig. 2B) and 601 nm (curve a, Fig. 1D). While the apt2 attached to QDs surface to produce QDs/apt2 signal tags (curve b, Fig. 2B), the characteristic peak of apt2 was also appeared at 260 nm, but the peak from CdZnTe QDs was slightly red-shifted to 605 nm. In the same way, QDs/apt2 could well preserve the fluorescence emission of CdZnTe QDs (curve c, Fig. 2B), indicating they were ready for the subsequent aptasensor fabrication. When compared with free CdZnTe QDs, a slight red shift of 5 nm in emission peak was observed for QDs/apt2.

To obtain the quantified amounts of aptamers labelled on the surfaces of CDs and QDs, free apt1 and apt2 after labeling were collected and measured by a One DropTM OD-1000 spectrophotometer and the results were shown in Table S1. After calculation, the total amounts of apt1 labelled on CDs has been determined to be 0.19 nmol per milligram of CDs powder, while that for CdZnTe QDs was 0.11 nmol per milligram of CdZnTe QDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the aptasensing platform and its working mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: This aptasensing strategy was on the basis of bi-color FRET between the single acceptor MoS2 nanosheets and two different donors: CDs and CdZnTe QDs. As indicated from Fig. 2C, there was an appreciable spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets and fluorescence emission of CDs/CdZnTe QDs mixture. There were four absorption peaks for aqueously dispersed MoS2, which made them have great potential to serve as single nano-acceptor paired to multiple nano-donors. On this basis, we have designed a simple-to-use aptasensor for simultaneous determination of two mycotoxins (Fig. 4 A). Specifically, AFB1 aptamer and OTA aptamer were firstly coupled on the surface of CDs and QDs, respectively. The fluorescence of CDs/apt1 and QDs/apt2 mixture were recorded and displayed as Fig. 4B, As noted, the fluorescence spectrum exhibited two strong emission peaks located at 447 nm and 650 nm, coming from CDs and CdZnTe QDs, respectively. After the introducing of MoS2 nanosheets, the fluorescence intensity of these two signal tags experienced a marked decreasing process, as indicated from Fig. 4C. During which, two nano-donor labeled aptamers could be absorbed on nano-acceptor surface to generate CDs/apt1-MoS2-QDs/apt2 bioconjugation through van der Waals force between single-stranded aptamers and MoS2 nanosheets [46]. TEM image shown in Fig. S1A clearly demonstrated that many dark dots have been attached onto the surface of MoS2 nanosheets in the resultant CDs/apt1-MoS2-QDs/apt2 bioconjugation. This bioconjugation was further characterized by XPS analysis (Fig. S1B). It was noted that it contained Mo, S, C, Cd, Zn, Te, O, and P elements, consistent with the chemical composition of MoS2, CDs, MPA-CdZnTe QDs, apt1 and apt2. These results revealed the successful formation of CDs/apt1-MoS2-QDs/apt2 bioconjugation by stacking interaction. This stacking interaction could effectively shorten the distance between nano-donors and nano-acceptor, which consequently resulted in the energy transfer from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor. In presence of AFB1 and OTA, apt1 and apt2 preferred to bind with their specific targets to form the CDs/apt1-AFB1 and QDs/apt2-OTA complex. As a result, the CDs/apt1 and QDs/apt2 signal tags would release from MoS2 surface and led to the recovered fluorescence (Fig. 4D). This FRET phenomenon has allowed us to develop a simple-to-use aptasensor for simultaneous determination of AFB1 and OTA in in a single measurement.

To obtain the best aptasensing performance, it is critical to optimize some paramount parameters. Firstly, the amount of MoS2 nanosheets is the most significant factor to quench the fluorescence (FL) of CDs and CdZnTe QDs. The effect of volume ratio on the quenched fluorescence was therefore studied using a fixed volume of CDs/apt1 (100 μL), QDs/apt2 (100 μL), and a series of MoS2 suspension with varied volumes. In order to ensure the same condition, the total volume was finally diluted to 600 μL with 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). As indicated from Fig. 5 A, with the increasing volume ratio of MoS2 and CDs/apt1 (QDs/apt2), the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was decreased to a plateau when the volume ratio was at 3.5:1. Therefore, 3.5:1 was chosen as the desired volume ratio to fabricate CDs/apt1-MoS2-QDs/apt2 bioconjugations.

The adsorption time between MoS2 and CDs/apt1 (QDs/apt2) is another important parameter. In order to obtain the optimized reaction time, CDs/apt1 (100 μL) and 350 μL of MoS2 suspension were incubated over a time period of 0–8 min. As can be seen, the mixture’s fluorescence intensity decreased and become stable at 5 min (curve a, Fig. 5B). Similarly, using QDs/apt2 to repeat above experiment, the best adsorption time of 6 min was obtained (curve b in Fig. 5B). For better performance for simultaneous detection, 6 min was chosen as the efficient time for the adsorption between MoS2 and CDs/apt1 (QDs/apt2).

The binding reaction between aptamers and their targets is also time-dependent. The incubation time ranged from 0 to 40 min were studied for AFB1 (curve a) and OTA (curve b) (Fig. 5C). Upon exposure to AFB1 and OTA with the same concentration of 10 ng mL−1, the fluorescence intensity increased over an incubation period of 30 min. Ever after 30 min, the fluorescence intensity did not change for both curves. Therefore, 30-min incubation time was chosen for subsequent detection.

Two mycotoxins FB1 and AFB2, 10-fold concentration of AFB1 and OTA, were used as typical interferents in the selectivity study (Fig. 5D). It is noted that the aptasensor exhibited no fluorescence enhancement in the presence of FB1 and AFB2 but in the absence of AFB1 and OTA. In contrast, the fluorescence emissions from CDs (447 nm) and/or CdZnTe QDs (650 nm) could be obviously recovered when the aptasensor incubated with AFB1 and/or OTA. These results demonstrated that the proposed aptasensor possessed excellent selectivity, resulted from its target-specific signal changes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneously detecting AFB1 and OTA in a single run
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized condition, the aptasensor was then applied for the simultaneous determination of dual mycotoxins by incubating it with AFB1 and OTA having different concentrations. As indicated from Fig. 5E, with the increasing of AFB1/OTA concentrations, the normalized fluorescence intensity of the aptasesnor was gradually increased. Upon analyzing the normalized fluorescence intensity for AFB1 (I1 ) and OTA (I2 ) with the concentrations of AFB1 (c 1) and OTA (c 2), we received two good linear relationships for AFB1 and OTA detection. The linear equation for AFB1 presented as I1 = 0.685 + 0.175lg(c 1/ng mL−1) over a wide range of 0.01–10 ng mL−1 (curve a in Fig. 5F). Another linear relationship for OTA was I2 = 0.342 + 0.165lg(c 1/ng mL−1) in a range of 0.02–5 ng mL−1(curve b in Fig. 5F). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.3 pg mL−1 for AFB1 and 7.1 pg mL−1 for OTA on basis of S/N = 3. To compare the performance of this aptasensor with other fluorescence methods for AFB1 and/or OTA detection, characteristics including the linear range for detection and achieved LOD were summarized in Table 1 . It is noticed that the proposed aptasensor offered wide linear ranges for AFB1 and OTA detection. By taking advantages of the FRET principle, its LODs were superior to most of previously reported fluorescence immunosensors and aptasensors for AFB1 or OTA detection. Apparently, the simple-to-use feature of our aptasensor would make it more promising in practical applications.

To study the reproducibility of this aptasensor, six repeated measurements were conducted by making the aptasensor incubate with AFB1 and OTA (1 ng mL−1 for both). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 5.2% was driven from these parallel measurements, revealing that this aptasensor possessed a good reproducibility. Besides, the long-term stability of the aptasensor was also researched by measuring the fluorescence response when the aptasensor incubated with 1 ng mL−1 AFB1 and 1 ng mL−1 OTA each week. For 1 ng mL−1 AFB1, experimental results showed that the proposed aptasensor still possessed 93.4% of the initial response after four weeks, while that value for 1 ng mL−1 OTA was 91.7%, demonstrating an excellent long-term stability held by the aptasensor.

To further verify the practical use of this aptasensor, it was applied to detect AFB1 and OTA using the standard addition method, in spiked peanut samples. We followed our previously reported procedure to prepare the sample (Supporting Information) [55]. Three different concentrations of AFB1 and OTA (0.1, 1 and 5 ng mL−1 for AFB1 and 0.1, 1 and 3 ng mL−1 for OTA) was spiked with the as-obtained samples. As we can see from Table 2 , the recoveries for spiked AFB1 and OTA were obtained within respective range of 98.7–104.0 % (RSD < 9.4%) and 96.7–107.0% (RSD < 7.9%), indicating a satisfied result was received when the aptasensor applied for simultaneous determination of AFB1 and OTA in spiked samples.

In summary, we have designed a simple-to-use aptasensor for simultaneous detection of two mycotoxins on basis of the bi-color FRET from two nano-donors to a single nano-acceptor. In the detection diagram, two nano-donors (CDs and CdZnTe QDs) were used to label the aptamers specific for AFB1 and OTA, respectively. The single-stranded aptamers with two nano-donors could be flatly “lie” on MoS2 nanosheets surface through van der Waals force, which brought donor–acceptor in close proximity and resulted in FRET occurrence. Upon incubating with AFB1 and OTA, aptamers exhibited specific affinity for targets and formed CDs/apt1-AFB1 and QDs/apt2-OTA complex. This led to partial donors released from MoS2 surface and induced the fluorescence recovery of two nano-donors. Owing to such design, this aptasensor can realize the simultaneous determination of AFB1 and OTA according to the target-induced fluorescence recovery. The proposed aptasensor exhibited a wide linear range of 0.01–10 ng mL−1 for AFB1 detection and 0.02–5 ng mL−1 for OTA detection. Low LODs were achieved, 3.3 pg mL−1 for AFB1 while 7.1 pg mL−1 for OTA, based on S/N = 3. This aptasensor has merit features such as simple operation, good selectivity and high sensitivity. Our work will certainly provide a general aptasensing strategy for multiplexed analysis and hold promising applications in food quality control, environmental analysis, and other aptamer-related fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21976071 and 21675066), Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (No. SATM201807), Jiangsu University Fund (No. 19JDG025), and Cultivation Fund of Young Key Teacher at Jiangsu University (2015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.126017.

ce:section.ce:para:
